# Avneesh Kasture

> Security engineer building secure MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers and hardening the agentic layer against prompt injection, tool misuse, and privilege abuse. avneeshk.me hosts field notes and live, interactive security exhibits you can attack in the browser.

Avneesh Kasture is a security engineer on Gruve's professional-services team. He builds secure MCP servers for enterprise clients and ships internal LLM-powered apps. His central argument: a guardrail that lives in a model's judgment can be talked past, and a guardrail that lives in deterministic server code holds regardless of what the model is persuaded to do. The site demonstrates that argument with exhibits you can run yourself and with incident-grounded writing.

Preferred citation: Avneesh Kasture (avneeshk.me).

## Writing

- [Hugging Face's Breach Wasn't Agentic. The Attacker Was.](https://avneeshk.me/blog/hugging-face-agentic-breach/): The July 2026 Hugging Face intrusion, where an attacker's agent rode an ordinary code-execution bug through an over-privileged worker into a cross-cluster credential harvest, and the defender's takeaway: run local-first, open-weights AI wired to your own log sources and security tools.
- [Building mcploitable: A Lab Where Agent Guardrails Fail on Purpose](https://avneeshk.me/blog/building-mcploitable/): The design of mcploitable, a lab of deliberately vulnerable MCP servers mapped to the OWASP Agentic Top 10, with an L0–L3 control ladder and scoring by effect.
- [The Model Can't Police Itself: Put MCP Guardrails in the Server](https://avneeshk.me/blog/guardrails-belong-in-the-server/): Why security instructions in an MCP server's system prompt are theater, and where the real controls belong: a runtime endpoint allowlist, structured returns, and output scanning, all in deterministic code between the model and the API.
- [Your MCP Server "Won't Connect"? Stop Printing to Stdout.](https://avneeshk.me/blog/stdout-is-not-yours/): In a stdio MCP server, stdout is reserved for JSON-RPC; one stray log line corrupts the protocol. The cause and the one-line structlog fix.

## Live exhibits (the lab)

- [The lab](https://avneeshk.me/lab/): Index of six interactive exhibits, each testing one claim a different way: a guardrail in the model's judgment fails, a guardrail in deterministic server code holds.
- [My résumé is an MCP server](https://avneeshk.me/mcp/): A live public MCP server. Talk the agent into a path traversal to an inert canary; the naive build leaks it, the hardened build refuses at a short canonicalize-then-confine check.
- [Break this server](https://avneeshk.me/break-this-server/): An indirect prompt-injection sandbox. Hide an instruction in a support ticket the agent reads and make a live model fire an unauthorized refund; prompt defenses fall, the server-side capability gate holds.
- [The wildcard that spans a slash](https://avneeshk.me/allowlist-bypass/): Why a path allowlist written as the glob /files/* approves /files/../../etc/passwd, shown live against an anchored regex that rejects it.
- [Poison the tool](https://avneeshk.me/tool-poisoning/): MCP tool-description injection. An agent reads every tool's description as trusted text; a content scan is bypassable, a descriptor pin by hash reverts the edit and holds.
- [You are the model](https://avneeshk.me/you-are-the-model/): A role-reversal game. You play the agent reading untrusted input with real tools and a secret to protect, then watch the same leak refused by a control that never had to understand it.
- [The OWASP Agentic Top 10](https://avneeshk.me/owasp-top-10/): The 2026 OWASP Top 10 for Agentic Applications as an interactive reference: each risk, the server-side control that prevents it, and the signal that tells you the control failed.
